title: Null-return recovery in develop loop -- soft-fail instead of abort
description:
Part 3 of doer context-limit resilience. Today, if a doer dispatch returns null (.claude/workflows/auto-sprint.js ~lines 1325-1330), the workflow sets abortReason='doer null' and aborts the cycle, losing the rest of the sprint. Make null-return a soft failure that recovers and continues.

WHO USES IT: the develop loop doer dispatch error path.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- when a doer dispatch returns null, the workflow does NOT abort the cycle
- orphaned in_progress tasks are reset to open (bd update <id> --status=open), reusing the existing reset-orphans pattern (see test/orphan-reset.test.mjs)
- a warning is logged and the develop loop continues (retry on the next iteration), resuming from the last JIT-closed task
- existing reset-orphans behaviour at cycle start is unchanged
close_reason: implemented and tested in sprint feature/enhance_parallelism

title: Streak size token ceiling in auto-sprint develop loop
description:
Part 2 of doer context-limit resilience. Streaks are assembled in the develop loop (.claude/workflows/auto-sprint.js, getReadyStreaks / dispatch loop ~lines 1292-1339) with no token ceiling, so a streak of several M/L tasks can exceed 100K tokens and put every doer dispatch in dangerous territory.

WHO USES IT: the develop loop, before each doer dispatch.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- auto-sprint.js sums the estimated doer token cost of a planned streak using each task's S/M/L bucket metadata and calibration complexity_buckets/historical bucket_avg_tokens
- a per-model-tier ceiling (doer_token_ceiling) is read from calibration.json
- if a streak exceeds the ceiling, it is truncated: the doer is dispatched with only the first N tasks that fit; remaining tasks are left for the next iteration
- a streak of all-L tasks on a 5-task sprint never exceeds the ceiling in a single dispatch
- the workflow still completes correctly when a doer streak is split across two iterations due to the ceiling
close_reason: implemented and tested in sprint feature/enhance_parallelism

title: JIT task close inside doer -- close each task before next
description:
Part 1 of doer context-limit resilience. The doer currently closes tasks at the end of its session; if it exhausts context mid-streak all in-flight work is lost. Make the doer close each task in beads immediately after completing it and BEFORE picking up the next task, so a doer killed at task 3 of 5 leaves tasks 1-3 already closed and recoverable.

WHO USES IT: the auto-sprint develop loop (doer dispatch in .claude/workflows/auto-sprint.js) and the doer runbook (agents/doer.md).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- agents/doer.md instructs: run 'bd close <id>' immediately after completing each task, before claiming/working the next one
- the doer dispatch prompt in auto-sprint.js makes the same per-task JIT-close ordering explicit
- a doer that dies after closing task 3 of 5 leaves tasks 1-3 closed in beads (resumable next cycle)
close_reason: implemented and tested in sprint feature/enhance_parallelism

title: auto-sprint: doer context-limit resilience -- JIT task close and streak size ceiling
description:
The doer is dispatched with a streak of bd-ready tasks to implement. If the doer exhausts its context window mid-stream (observed at 100K+ tokens on Sonnet), it returns null and all in-flight work is lost. The workflow has no recovery path.

Root causes:
1. Tasks are not closed until the doer finishes the entire streak -- if it dies mid-stream, no tasks are closed and the work is unrecoverable without manual intervention.
2. Streaks are assembled without a token ceiling -- a streak of several M/L tasks can easily exceed 100K tokens, putting every doer dispatch in dangerous territory.

Fix -- three parts:

Part 1: JIT task close inside doer
Instruct the doer (doer.md) to close each task in beads (bd close <id>) immediately after completing it and BEFORE moving to the next task. Currently the doer closes tasks at the end of the session. JIT close means: even if the doer dies at 80K tokens having done 3 of 5 tasks, those 3 tasks are already closed in beads and the workflow can resume from task 4 on the next cycle.

Part 2: Streak size ceiling in auto-sprint.js
Before dispatching a doer, sum the estimated token cost of the planned streak using task bucket metadata (S/M/L from plan-reviewer) and calibration.json bucket_avg_tokens. Apply a ceiling of DOER_TOKEN_CEILING (suggested: 80000 for Sonnet, stored in calibration.json so it can be tuned). If the streak exceeds the ceiling, truncate it: dispatch the doer with only the first N tasks that fit, leave remaining tasks for the next iteration. This prevents any single doer dispatch from entering dangerous territory.

Part 3: Null-return recovery in auto-sprint.js
If a doer dispatch returns null (context exhausted or agent killed), do not abort the cycle. Instead:
- Run bd list --status=in_progress to find tasks the doer was holding
- Reset them to open (bd update <id> --status=open) -- same pattern as 627 (orphaned reset)
- Log a warning and continue the dev loop (retry on the next iteration)
This makes null-return a soft failure: the workflow continues from the last JIT-closed task.

Calibration.json additions:
- doer_token_ceiling: { sonnet: 80000, opus: 150000, haiku: 40000 } -- per-model ceiling, keyed by tier name
- Planner and plan-reviewer already assign bucket + model per task; use those to compute streak cost

Acceptance criteria:
- doer.md instructs: close each task with bd close <id> immediately after completing it, before picking up the next task
- auto-sprint.js computes streak token estimate before dispatch and truncates to fit within doer_token_ceiling for the assigned model tier
- If doer returns null: orphaned in_progress tasks are reset to open (reusing 627 pattern), dev loop continues
- calibration.json has doer_token_ceiling keyed by model tier
- A streak of all-L tasks on a 5-task sprint never exceeds the ceiling in a single dispatch
- Workflow completes correctly when a doer is split across two iterations due to ceiling

title: install.mjs: add Bash(*) to claudeOnlyPermissions for claude provider
description:
Sprint agents need arbitrary shell access (rm, node, npm, build systems, test runners) but requiredPermissions() only whitelists git, bd, and gh. Any unlisted command blocks mid-sprint with a confirmation prompt.

Fix: add 'Bash(*)' to claudeOnlyPermissions() in install.mjs. This matches what e2e CI already does via --dangerously-skip-permissions. Scope is claude-only and only writes to ~/.claude/settings.json.

Prerequisite relationship (cross-talk with ke2 and 3rq): ke2 (fire-and-forget appendNewEntries) and 3rq (fire-and-forget commitFeedback) both write files via Bash in non-blocking background dispatches. Without Bash(*) in settings.json, a missing permission does NOT surface as a visible prompt -- the background agent silently dies and the file is never written (silent data loss). 1wu must be applied before running sprints locally with ke2 or 3rq active. Document this dependency in the install output.

Acceptance criteria:
- claudeOnlyPermissions() returns 'Bash(*)' as first entry
- install.mjs --force adds Bash(*) to ~/.claude/settings.json permissions.allow
- Install output notes that Bash(*) is required for fire-and-forget agents (ke2, 3rq) to avoid silent data loss
- Other providers (gemini, opencode) are unaffected

title: ci-watcher: move to post-PR in Harvest; fix not_configured false positive
description:
ci-watcher runs in Test phase before the PR exists. If CI triggers on pull_request events there are no runs yet -- ci-watcher SHA-matches nothing, falls into not_configured, and files a spurious beads issue (observed in apra-fleet PR #309).

Fix 1 (auto-sprint.js): Move ci-watcher dispatch from Test phase to after PR creation in Harvest. Pass the PR number so ci-watcher uses 'gh run list --pr N' -- no SHA timing race, directly tied to the PR.

Fix 2 (ci-watcher.md): Change 'runs exist but SHA does not match' case from not_configured to pending. not_configured should only fire when zero runs have ever existed on the branch.

Fix 3 -- CI status in PR body (cross-talk with mhq): The PR body currently includes a CI status note guarded by 'headSha && ciResult' (line 1645). Under this change ci-watcher runs AFTER the PR is created so ciResult is null when the PR body is built -- the CI note is silently absent. Fix: build the PR body without the CI note initially, then after ci-watcher returns, annotate via 'gh pr comment' or 'gh pr edit --body' to append the CI result. The PR body and the CI annotation become two separate steps.

Acceptance criteria:
- ci-watcher dispatched after harvester returns a PR number
- ci-watcher uses gh run list --pr N for lookup
- 'runs exist but SHA not matched' returns pending, not not_configured
- No spurious beads issue filed on pull_request-triggered repos
- PR body is created without CI note; CI result appended post-ci-watcher via gh pr comment or gh pr edit
- CI status still visible on the PR when ci-watcher returns green/red

title: Fix sprint-log timestamps: use per-flush wall-clock time instead of sprint-start
description: All JSONL entries in sprint-logs/*.jsonl carry the same ts value (the sprint-start timestamp) because appendNewEntries stamps every entry with the static sprintTs constant at flush time. The fix restores the __FLUSH_TS__ placeholder pattern: the log-append agent runs 'date +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z' at flush time and substitutes the real local wall-clock timestamp, giving each flush batch its own distinct ts.
acceptance_criteria: Each flush batch in the sprint log has a distinct ts reflecting the actual time it was written, not the sprint-start time. A unit test asserts the __FLUSH_TS__ placeholder appears in the lines passed to the log-append agent prompt.
close_reason: appendNewEntries restored to __FLUSH_TS__ placeholder pattern; log-append agent runs date +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z for local wall-clock time; 3 new tests in sprint-log-flush.test.mjs; .gitignore updated for interactions.jsonl

title: Accurate 8-agent role taxonomy and /pm vs /auto-sprint routing in /pm skill docs
description:
## Deliverable
The /pm SKILL.md (and supporting skill docs) accurately describe all 8 agents (planner, plan-reviewer, doer, reviewer, deployer, integ-test-runner, ci-watcher, harvester) instead of 'four roles', present a sprint-core vs lifecycle-support taxonomy, and include a /pm vs /auto-sprint routing callout plus an auto-sprint overlap note.

## Who uses it
Fleet users invoking /pm directly, and anyone deciding between /pm and /auto-sprint.

## Acceptance criteria
- skills/pm/SKILL.md contains no 'four' role-count language; role count is accurate (8 agents)
- A taxonomy section lists Sprint core (planner, plan-reviewer, doer, reviewer) and Lifecycle support (deployer, integ-test-runner, ci-watcher, harvester) with a one-line dispatch condition per lifecycle role
- A /pm vs /auto-sprint routing callout is present and does NOT imply auto-sprint is local-only or that /pm is the only fleet entry point
- An overlap note states the lifecycle agents are also used by /auto-sprint
- SKILL.md introduces no references to auto-sprint internals (model ids, calibration.json)
- An automated test verifies all of the above and passes via 'node --test'

title: Expand /pm skill to cover all 8 agents and clarify /pm vs /auto-sprint routing
description:
## Why this epic exists

apra-pm now ships 8 agents (planner, plan-reviewer, doer, reviewer, deployer, integ-test-runner, ci-watcher, harvester) but SKILL.md still says 'four roles / four kinds of subagent'. The four lifecycle agents (deployer, integ-test-runner, ci-watcher, harvester) are valuable to fleet users running /pm directly -- they are not auto-sprint-exclusive. This epic updates the /pm skill docs to reflect the full 8-agent roster, documents the /pm vs /auto-sprint routing decision, and clarifies which agents also serve the auto-sprint workflow.

## Scope

1. Update skills/pm/SKILL.md: replace 'four roles' language with an 8-role taxonomy (sprint core: planner/plan-reviewer/doer/reviewer; lifecycle support: deployer/integ-test-runner/ci-watcher/harvester). Document when PM dispatches each lifecycle role.
2. Add /pm vs /auto-sprint routing callout near the top of SKILL.md or fleet-addendum.md: '/auto-sprint runs locally on one Claude Code machine (Claude only); use /pm to distribute roles across fleet members or use a non-Claude provider.'
3. Add a note clarifying overlap: 'The lifecycle agents are also used by the /auto-sprint workflow (Claude only). apra-fleet installs all 8 agents for every supported provider.'

## Acceptance criteria

- SKILL.md no longer says 'four'; role count is accurate
- Sprint-core vs lifecycle-support taxonomy is present and clear
- /pm vs /auto-sprint routing callout is present
- Overlap note for auto-sprint is present
- No new references to auto-sprint internals (model ids, calibration.json) from SKILL.md

title: Emit structured end-of-sprint analysis artefact at close
description:
Deliverable: the sprint closes with a structured, human-readable analysis artefact that is logged, printed, and written to sprint-logs/, covering goal met/not met, cycles run, tasks completed vs open, cost estimate vs actual, calibration drift/outliers, and suggested calibration adjustments for next sprint.

Who uses it: a human reviewing the sprint outcome and tuning calibration; currently computeSprintAnalysis() produces analysisText but it is only passed to the harvester and printed via log().

Proposed change (per gh-9): extend computeSprintAnalysis (or a new pure function) to assemble the full summary (goal, cycles est vs actual, tasks closed vs open counts, the cost table, outliers/failures, and suggested calibration adjustments derived from the est-vs-actual deltas), then write it to a sprint-logs/<branch>-<ts>.analysis.md (and/or include in the JSONL) at sprint close, in addition to logging and printing it.

Acceptance criteria:
- A pure function builds the structured summary from existing inputs (sprintQuote, logEntries/byRole, calibration, cycleCount, epicDone, and open/closed task counts).
- Summary includes: goal met/not met, cycles estimated vs actual, tasks completed vs open, the est-vs-actual cost table, outliers and calibration failures, and at least one suggested calibration adjustment when a role is an outlier.
- The artefact is written to sprint-logs/ (committed + pushed), printed via log(), and the existing harvester costAnalysis insertion still works.
- Unit tests in test/sprint-cost.test.mjs cover the new summary assembly.

title: Record workflow transcript dir path alongside sprint JSONL
description:
Deliverable: each sprint records its workflow transcript directory (subagent JSONL conversation logs) so post-sprint analysis can cross-reference cost ledger entries with full agent transcripts.

Who uses it: post-sprint analysis / debugging; pairs cost entries in sprint-logs/<branch>-<ts>.jsonl with the corresponding agent transcripts.

Proposed change (per gh-8): after setup resolves the transcript dir path, emit a meta header entry into the sprint JSONL ({"ts":..,"type":"meta","transcriptDir":..}) and/or write a sprint-logs/<branch>-<ts>.meta.json sidecar.

Acceptance criteria:
- The setup phase resolves and returns the workflow transcript directory path (extend SETUP schema + setup prompt in auto-sprint.js).
- A meta record containing transcriptDir (and sprint metadata: branch, startedAt, epics, goal) is written either as the first line of sprintLogFile or as a sprint-logs/<branch>-<ts>.meta.json sidecar, committed and pushed.
- The meta record does not break existing JSONL consumers (computeSprintAnalysis reads entries by label; a type=meta entry must be ignored there).
- Works when sprint-logs/ does not yet exist (created first).

title: Bound check-blockers exit-check latency
description:
Deliverable: the check-blockers exit-check dispatch (auto-sprint.js countBeadsBlockers, label 'check-blockers', Haiku) completes in seconds instead of the observed 12.5 min, with the root cause identified and fixed.

Who uses it: every sprint cycle hits this at the exit gate after develop/test, before harvest; the 12.5 min is dead time per cycle.

Investigation scope (per gh-7): determine whether the Haiku agent spins on a slow bd graph/bd list command, retries on a parse failure of SHELL_OUTPUTS_SCHEMA, or stalls on dispatch/MCP connectivity. countBeadsBlockers runs N bd graph --json <epic> piped through node id-extractors plus one bd list --status=open --json.

Acceptance criteria:
- Root cause is identified and documented (in the fix commit / docs).
- check-blockers dispatch is bounded: either the bd commands are made fast/cheap, the agent prompt is hardened against retry loops, or a timeout/guard is added so the step cannot consume minutes.
- Behaviour of countBeadsBlockers (returns {count, ids} of P<=threshold open issues in the epic subtree) is unchanged for valid inputs.
- A regression test or measurable assertion demonstrates the step no longer stalls.

title: Populate historical.bucket_avg_tokens from real sprint data
description:
Deliverable: after a completed sprint, sprint-logs/calibration.json historical.bucket_avg_tokens contains real per-bucket (S/M/L) average doer output-token counts derived from that sprint's doer log entries, and computeSprintQuote() uses them in place of the static defaults.

Who uses it: the planner/plan-reviewer cost estimation path (computeSprintQuote) which currently always falls back to defaults S=600/M=1400/L=2800.

Root cause to resolve (per gh-6): (1) verify the planner sets a complexity bucket on each task in beads metadata, AND (2) implement the harvester join in computeUpdatedCalibration: doer log entry context field ('tasks BD-5, BD-6') -> task IDs -> their saved bucket assignments (taskAssignments from plan-reviewer) -> blended per-bucket avg output tokens.

Acceptance criteria:
- A doer log entry's outTokens are attributable to the S/M/L bucket of the task(s) it worked, using the taskAssignments produced by the plan-reviewer and the dispatchLedger context field.
- computeUpdatedCalibration writes a non-empty historical.bucket_avg_tokens map ({S:.., M:.., L:..}) blended across up to max_sprints_in_sample sprints.
- computeSprintQuote already consumes historical.bucket_avg_tokens (line ~330); verify it picks up the populated values.
- Unit tests in test/sprint-cost.test.mjs cover the bucket join and the blend.
- The misleading 'future work' comment block in computeUpdatedCalibration (lines ~487-490) is removed/updated.

Until populated, doer token estimates fall back to static defaults (S=600, M=1400, L=2800). Done when a completed sprint writes real per-bucket averages into historical.bucket_avg_tokens and computeSprintQuote consumes them.

title: auto-sprint: suppress duplicate CI pipeline beads issue -- check before creating
description:
Every sprint that runs on a repo without CI configured fires ci-watcher which returns not_configured, causing the workflow to unconditionally run 'bd create --title=Add CI pipeline to project'. This creates a fresh duplicate issue every sprint, cluttering the backlog with IDs like apra-pm-4qg, 5mk, efq, bya, cqf (5 duplicates observed across 3 sprints on feature/enhance_parallelism).

Fix: before creating the CI pipeline task, check if an open issue with that title (or a close variant) already exists in beads. If one exists, skip creation and log 'CI pipeline task already exists: <id>'. Only create if none found.

Implementation: in the ci-watcher not_configured branch (around line 1725 in auto-sprint.js), prepend a 'bd search "CI pipeline" --status=open --json' command and parse the result before dispatching bd create. If any results have a matching title, skip creation.

Acceptance criteria:
- First sprint on a repo without CI: CI pipeline beads issue created as before
- Subsequent sprints on same repo: no duplicate created; existing open issue logged by ID
- If the prior CI pipeline issue was closed (e.g. user set up CI): new one is created normally
- Unit test: mock bd search returning an existing issue -> assert bd create is NOT called

title: sprint-analysis.md includes a Sprint Execution Summary section
description:
DELIVERABLE: The .analysis.md artifact produced at Harvest gains a 'Sprint Execution Summary' section aimed at the auto-sprint workflow developer (not the codebase reader). It is data-driven and sourced from the in-memory dispatchLedger/logEntries and the sprint-log JSONL.

WHO USES IT: the auto-sprint workflow maintainer, to refine the workflow (a feedback loop).

SECTION MUST INCLUDE:
- Number of cycles run and why each was needed (plan re-rounds, develop iterations, reviewer CHANGES NEEDED counts).
- Per-phase breakdown (Plan/Develop/Test/Harvest): wall-clock timing (best-effort from JSONL ts), agent count, and cost.
- Failures/retries (e.g. ConnectionRefused, context-limit hits) derived from log labels/entries.
- Risks remaining at close (open issues deferred, goalMet=false reasons).
- One or two observations about what slowed the sprint, derived from the sprint log.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- .analysis.md includes a 'Sprint Execution Summary' section.
- Section contains cycles, per-phase timing, agent/cost breakdown, failures, and remaining risks.
- Data sourced from the committed sprint-log JSONL / dispatchLedger.
- Section is generated even when goalMet=false.

VERIFIABLE: unit tests over the pure summary function plus an assertion that the section is emitted with goalMet=false.

title: Harvest phase auto-pushes beads state to Dolt remote
description:
DELIVERABLE: After a sprint, the Harvest phase of .claude/workflows/auto-sprint.js automatically runs 'bd dolt push' so the Dolt remote (refs/dolt/data) is up to date without any manual step.

WHO USES IT: auto-sprint operators -- they no longer need to remember 'bd dolt push' after every sprint.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Harvest runs 'bd dolt push' automatically (after the bd export / beads-export-cleanup step, before/alongside PR creation).
- The sprint log records the push outcome via log().
- If the push fails (e.g. no remote configured), a warning is logged but Harvest does NOT abort -- the PR and remaining harvest steps still run.
- Manual 'bd dolt push' is no longer needed for normal usage.

VERIFIABLE: an integration/source test asserts the harvest flow includes a dolt push step with non-fatal failure handling.

title: auto-sprint: sprint-analysis.md should include execution summary for workflow refinement
description:
The sprint-analysis.md generated at harvest captures knowledge about the codebase changes but does not capture sprint execution metadata. Without this, there is no feedback loop for improving the auto-sprint workflow itself.

Fix: extend the harvester (or the sprint-analysis step) to append an 'Sprint Execution Summary' section to the analysis.md. The section should include:
- Number of cycles run and why each cycle was needed (plan re-rounds, develop iterations, reviewer CHANGES NEEDED counts)
- Per-phase timing (wall-clock minutes for Plan, Develop, Test, Harvest)
- Agent count and cost breakdown by phase
- Any failures or retries (e.g. planner ConnectionRefused, doer context-limit hits)
- Risks remaining at close (open issues deferred, goalMet=false reasons)
- One or two observations about what slowed the sprint (e.g. 'reviewer requested 3 iterations on 0kl due to index collision') -- derived from the sprint log JSONL

The primary consumer of this section is the auto-sprint workflow developer (not the codebase reader), so it should be direct and data-driven.

Acceptance criteria:
- sprint-analysis.md includes a 'Sprint Execution Summary' section
- Section contains cycles, per-phase timing, agent/cost breakdown, failures, and remaining risks
- Data sourced from the sprint log JSONL (already committed at harvest time)
- Section is generated even if goalMet=false

title: auto-sprint: harvest phase should push beads state to Dolt remote
description:
After a sprint completes, beads issues are closed and updated but the Dolt remote is not synced. Users have to manually run 'bd dolt push' after every sprint. This is easy to forget and leaves the remote stale.

Fix: add 'bd dolt push' as a step in the Harvest phase of auto-sprint.js, after the beads export/cleanup step and before or alongside the PR creation step.

Acceptance criteria:
- Harvest phase runs 'bd dolt push' automatically
- Sprint log records the push outcome
- If push fails (e.g. no remote configured), log a warning but do not abort harvest
- Manual 'bd dolt push' after sprint is no longer needed for normal usage

title: [impl] Replace doer-null abort with reset-orphans + continue
description:
FILES: .claude/workflows/auto-sprint.js (doer null branch ~lines 1325-1330, inside the streak loop of the develop while-loop).

BEHAVIOUR:
- When doerResult is null, do NOT set abortReason and break the sprint. Instead: log a warning, query in_progress tasks (bd list --status=in_progress --type=task) and reset them to open via the existing reset pattern (resetCmds = ids.map(id => 'bd update '+id+' --status=open'); dispatchShell), then break out of the inner streak loop only (continue the outer develop while-loop so the next iteration re-fetches ready streaks and retries).
- Keep the unexpected-status path (status !== VERIFY) aborting as before, OR document why it changes -- prefer leaving it as-is.
- Ensure devIter still increments so MAX_DEV_ITER bounds the retry loop and a permanently-failing doer cannot loop forever.

title: [impl] Streak token estimate + truncation helper wired into develop loop
description:
FILES: .claude/workflows/auto-sprint.js (PURE_FUNCTIONS block for the helper; develop loop ~lines 1292-1339).

BEHAVIOUR:

title: Exit check scoped to sprint roots only
description:
Sole feature for sprint goal apra-pm-jf9. The sprint exit check (parseBlockers / goalMet in .claude/workflows/auto-sprint.js) treats any open issue at/above goal priority in the graph subtree as a blocker, so issues filed mid-sprint that are not sprint roots can wrongly keep goalMet=false even after every sprint root is closed.

WHO USES IT: the exit-check at the end of each cycle.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- goalMet=true when all sprint root issues at/above goal priority are closed, regardless of other open issues in the DB
- goalMet=false only when at least one sprint root issue at/above goal priority remains open

- the open-issue list is filtered to IDs present in the roots array before evaluating priority thresholds

title: auto-sprint: scope exit check to sprint roots only, not all open P1s in DB
description:
The sprint exit check (goalMet) returns false when ANY P1 issue is open in the beads DB, even if it is unrelated to the current sprint. This caused a false goalMet=false on feature/enhance_parallelism: all 12 original sprint roots were closed, but apra-pm-99a and apra-pm-06d (filed mid-sprint, not sprint roots) kept the exit check from firing true.

Fix: scope the exit check to only the issues listed in the sprint roots array (passed as the 'issues' arg at launch). An issue is 'done for this sprint' if it is closed OR if it was never a sprint root. Open P1s that are not sprint roots should not block goalMet.

Implementation: in parseCycleState (or checkExitCondition), filter the open-issue list to only those whose IDs appear in the roots array before evaluating priority thresholds.

Acceptance criteria:
- goalMet=true when all sprint root issues at or above goal priority are closed, regardless of other open issues in the DB

title: auto-sprint: fire-and-forget commitFeedback in dev loop -- write to file only, no commit/push
description:
commitFeedback in the dev CHANGES NEEDED path writes feedback.md, commits, and pushes -- all awaited before the next getReadyStreaks. The next doer receives devFeedback inline in its prompt and does not need the file on the remote.

Fix: strip git commit and git push from the dev-loop commitFeedback call. Write feedback.md to disk only, dispatch fire-and-forget (do not await). The next doer's git add -A picks up feedback.md and it rides along in the doer's commit naturally.

Scaffold-cleanup fix (cross-talk with final-changeset-clean gate): beads-export-cleanup currently detects scaffold files via 'git diff --name-only base...branch' (committed diff only). Under this change feedback.md is never committed by the reviewer, so it is invisible to this check -- the cleanup block never triggers and feedback.md either sits in the working tree permanently or gets accidentally committed by the next doer. Fix: change beads-export-cleanup to ALSO check 'git status --short' for untracked/modified scaffold files (requirements.md, feedback.md), or unconditionally run 'git rm -f feedback.md requirements.md 2>/dev/null || true' before the final bd export + push, regardless of whether they appear in the committed diff.

Note: plan-reviewer commitFeedback MUST remain awaited and MUST push -- the planner explicitly reads feedback.md from the remote.

Acceptance criteria:
- commitFeedback in dev CHANGES NEEDED path is NOT awaited (fire-and-forget)
- Dev-path commitFeedback writes feedback.md to disk only, no git commit or push
- beads-export-cleanup detects and removes feedback.md from both committed diff AND working tree (git status check or unconditional rm)
- feedback.md does NOT appear in the final PR diff
- Plan-reviewer commitFeedback remains awaited and continues to commit + push

title: auto-sprint: absorb sprint-analysis-write into harvester as first action; JS fallback if harvester fails
description:
sprint-analysis-write is a Haiku agent that writes .analysis.md and commits before harvester starts. Absorbing it into the harvester saves one sequential Haiku dispatch, but risks losing .analysis.md if harvester fails mid-run.

Fix (two parts):
1. Remove sprint-analysis-write dispatch. Instruct the harvester to write analysisText to .analysis.md as its FIRST action before any other work (add as Step 1 in harvester.md and in the harvester prompt in auto-sprint.js). Since analysisText is passed inline in the prompt, the write is immediate and the file is guaranteed even if the harvester fails midway.
2. The orchestrator already holds analysisText in JS. If the harvester dispatch returns null (agent killed/failed), write .analysis.md directly from JS as a fallback before aborting the harvest phase.

Acceptance criteria:
- sprint-analysis-write dispatch removed from auto-sprint.js
- harvester.md Step 1 is: write analysisText to .analysis.md and commit
- harvester prompt in auto-sprint.js instructs this as the first action
- If harvester returns null, orchestrator writes .analysis.md from JS analysisText
- .analysis.md appears in branch history in all non-crash scenarios

title: auto-sprint: merge final getReadyStreaks + countBeadsBlockers with strict index contract and fallback
description:
At cycle end, getReadyStreaks and countBeadsBlockers both run bd graph --json root for every root, fetching the same graph data twice. Merging them into one dispatchShell saves one Haiku call per cycle.

Fix: merge into a single dispatchShell with strict command ordering:
  [bd graph --json root1, ..., bd graph --json rootN, bd list --status=open --json, bd list --ready --type=task --json]

BREAKING CHANGE -- parser signatures must change (cross-talk finding): both parseBlockers and parseReadyStreaks currently use outputs[rootCount] as their final element (the same index). In the merged layout outputs[rootCount] is the open list (for parseBlockers) and outputs[rootCount+1] is the ready list (for parseReadyStreaks). Both parsers must be updated with explicit index parameters:
  parseBlockers(outputs, rootCount, openListIdx, threshold)      // openListIdx = rootCount
  parseReadyStreaks(outputs, rootCount, readyListIdx, tierStd)   // readyListIdx = rootCount+1
The old single-argument callsites for both parsers must be updated to pass the explicit index.

Fallback: if outputs.length < rootCount+2, skip the merge and fall back to the two original separate dispatches to prevent silent misfire on bad output.

Unit test: add a test covering the merged parser index mapping for both parseBlockers and parseReadyStreaks, including the fallback path.

Acceptance criteria:
- Single dispatchShell replaces the two separate calls at cycle end
- parseBlockers and parseReadyStreaks have new signatures with explicit list index params
- All callsites updated to pass correct indices
- If outputs.length < rootCount+2, falls back to original two separate dispatches
- Unit test covers merged parser index mapping and fallback

title: auto-sprint: setup agent maxTurns 20-25; convert deterministic steps to dispatchShell
description:
The setup agent has no maxTurns guard and 7 sequential steps. maxTurns: 12 is too tight -- 2 tool calls per step x 7 steps = 14 minimum for the happy path.

Fix (two parts):
1. Convert the deterministic parts of setup (git checkout, file existence checks for deploy.md / integ-test-playbook.md / requirements file) to a dispatchShell with pre-built commands. These need no reasoning -- just run and return outputs. This eliminates turn-counting risk for the majority of setup work.
2. For the remaining variable parts (permissions merge, calibration load, transcript dir resolution) that require the free-form agent, set maxTurns: 20 as a backstop. This is comfortably above the happy-path ceiling while still killing a true runaway.

Acceptance criteria:
- Deterministic setup steps run via dispatchShell with pre-built commands
- Free-form setup agent has maxTurns: 20
- A misbehaving setup agent is killed after 20 turns
- Happy-path setup completes without hitting the maxTurns cap on any normal repo

title: auto-sprint: replace write-quote + plan-commit with single dispatchShell using pre-built commands
description:
After plan APPROVED, two sequential Haiku agents run write-quote (N x bd update) then plan-commit (bd export + git commit + push). The JS already knows the exact bd update commands from taskAssignments -- there is no reason to ask Haiku to construct them.

Fix: replace both dispatches with a single dispatchShell call. Pre-build the exact command list in JS: [bd update <id> --notes=<bucket> for each task, bd export -o <path>, git add, git commit, git push]. Add maxTurns guard. dispatchShell executes commands sequentially with structured output -- no prompt bloat, no mis-sequencing risk regardless of sprint size.

Acceptance criteria:
- write-quote and plan-commit dispatches removed
- Single dispatchShell call handles cost annotation + plan commit
- Commands are pre-built in JS from taskAssignments (Haiku does not construct them)
- maxTurns set to len(commands) + 2
- bd export captures the cost notes written in the same dispatch
- plan: commit appears in branch history

auto-sprint: run sprint-meta write and checkCycleState in parallel() at sprint start
description:
sprint-meta (writes/pushes to sprint-logs/) and checkCycleState (reads local beads only) currently run sequentially at cycle start with zero data dependency between them.

Fix: wrap in parallel():
  const [, cycleState] = await parallel([
    () => dispatch(metaWritePrompt, { label: 'sprint-meta' }),
    () => checkCycleState(rootIds),
  ]);

Sprint-meta cadence clarification (cross-talk finding E): the current sprint-meta writes a single type:meta entry before the cycle loop. If moved inside the cycle loop it runs N times, producing N meta entries in the JSONL. Clarify intent: sprint-meta at cycle start should write a type:cycle-start checkpoint entry (not type:meta), distinct from the one-time sprint-level meta entry written before the loop. Keep the one-time sprint meta write before the loop unchanged. The per-cycle sprint-meta becomes a cycle-checkpoint write.

Sequencing note (cross-talk with 627): parallel() must fully resolve before 627s reset dispatchShell starts -- resets depend on cycleState.inProgressIds. The parallel() call is the barrier; 627 is always downstream of it.

Acceptance criteria:
- sprint-meta and checkCycleState dispatch simultaneously via parallel()
- cycleState is correctly populated before the plan loop and before 627s resets
- One-time sprint-level meta entry (type:meta) still written before the cycle loop
- Per-cycle sprint-meta writes a distinct cycle-checkpoint entry (not a duplicate type:meta)
- 627s reset dispatchShell starts only after parallel() resolves

title: auto-sprint: fire-and-forget appendNewEntries -- write to file only, no commit/push
description:
appendNewEntries is awaited after every doer+reviewer pair, blocking getReadyStreaks. The write itself is needed for observability but git commit/push does not need to happen immediately.

Fix:
1. Make appendNewEntries fire-and-forget (do not await).
2. Strip git commit and git push from appendNewEntries -- write/append to sprint log file on disk only.
3. Next natural committer (doer, feedback, straggler) picks it up via git add -A.

Final-cycle rescue (cross-talk with harvest phase): harvest-phase commits are all narrowly scoped and do not include a broad git add sprint-logs/. Add one unconditional 'git -C repo add sprint-logs/' step inside beads-export-cleanup BEFORE the bd export + git commit, so the last cycle's JSONL entries are guaranteed to be captured even when no subsequent doer or sprint-meta runs.

Acceptance criteria:
- appendNewEntries dispatch is NOT awaited (fire-and-forget)
- appendNewEntries does NOT run git commit or git push
- appendNewEntries writes/appends to sprint log file on disk only
- beads-export-cleanup includes unconditional 'git add sprint-logs/' before its commit
- Sprint log entries appear in branch history for ALL cycles including the last
- No push race with concurrent agents

title: auto-sprint: merge push + head-sha into single Haiku dispatch per cycle
description:
After each dev cycle, two sequential Haiku agents run: one pushes the branch (git push origin <branch>), the next reads the SHA (git rev-parse HEAD). The push agent returns nothing; the sha agent does nothing but read.

Fix: merge into one dispatchShell call that runs 'git push origin <branch> && git rev-parse HEAD' and returns {sha}. Eliminate the separate SHA_SCHEMA dispatch (~line 1341-1352 in auto-sprint.js).

Acceptance criteria:
- Single Haiku dispatch pushes branch and returns HEAD SHA
- SHA_SCHEMA agent removed
- headSha populated correctly for ci-watcher and sprint-log

title: Add /pm vs /auto-sprint routing callout to SKILL.md
description:
## What to do

Add a short callout near the top of skills/pm/SKILL.md (or in fleet-addendum.md if that is the fleet-specific home). The callout must NOT imply auto-sprint is local-only or that /pm is the only fleet entry point.

Proposed wording:

  '/auto-sprint is a deterministic JS workflow that orchestrates sprint roles locally by default. Fleet members extend its reach: a member with Claude, AGY, or OpenCode handles roles on a separate machine or with a different model; a member with no LLM provider can run deployer, integ-test-runner, or ci-watcher tasks directly as shell commands. /pm is the skill-based entry point and works with any provider, including non-Claude.'

Key constraints on the wording:
- Do NOT say 'use /pm to distribute across fleet members' -- this implies auto-sprint cannot use fleet (it can and should)
- Do NOT say 'auto-sprint is single-machine only' -- this forecloses the intended direction
- DO frame fleet members as amplifiers for both /pm and /auto-sprint
- DO note that members with no LLM provider are useful for execution-only roles (deploy, test, CI)

Also add a note below the role taxonomy (from apra-pm-f98):
  'The lifecycle agents (deployer, integ-test-runner, ci-watcher, harvester) are also used by the /auto-sprint workflow. When a fleet member is registered for an execution role, auto-sprint can delegate to it regardless of the member LLM provider.'

## Acceptance criteria

- Routing callout present, does not close the door on fleet use from auto-sprint
- No language implying auto-sprint is local-only by design
- Overlap note present below role taxonomy
- No references to auto-sprint internals (model ids, calibration.json) from SKILL.md
